title: 9.6 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 9.6!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best defines the concept of *transnationalism*?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Transnationalism describes connections that cross national borders.
Transnationalism means loyalty or devotion to one nation-state.
Transnationalism is nationalism based on shared race or ethnicity.
Transnationalism refers to fear or prejudice toward people from other nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
A majority of the world’s countries require at least one parent to be a citizen in order for a newborn to obtain citizenship. 

**How does the United States’ practice of birthright citizenship compare?** 


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Anyone born in the United States is granted citizenship, regardless of their parents' status.
The United States follows the same citizenship policy used by most countries.
Both parents must be United States citizens for the newborn to be a citizen.
Newborns are not considered U.S. citizens until they are issued a Social Security number.

assessment_items.question_content:
Scholars studying transnational white supremacist movements note that these groups operate across borders despite promoting nationalism. 

**Which conclusion best explains a shared feature of these movements worldwide?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They promote hostility toward immigrants and refugees, whom they view as threats to racial or cultural purity.
They demonstrate loyalty to the same nation-state and work together to expand its political influence.
They promote racial diversity as a way to strengthen national identity.
They rely on the same national symbols, such as the Confederate flag, to represent their movement globally.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What does it mean for a group to consider itself a people without a state?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They share a collective identity but do not have a sovereign territory of their own.
It refers to nationalism based on shared ethnicity rather than a nation-state.
It means the group identifies primarily as part of a global or international community.
It is the belief that personal identity does not depend on national identity.

assessment_items.question_content:
United Nations (UN) peacekeeping forces were present in both Bosnia and Rwanda when mass violence occurred, yet they were unable to stop the killings.

**What institutional weakness of the United Nations is most clearly revealed by these failures?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The United Nations must rely on member states to provide troops and resources to act.
The United Nations lacks an agreed-upon definition of genocide to guide when intervention is required.
The United Nations must wait for complete media verification before responding to reports of mass violence.
The United Nations regularly ignores international law when responding to global conflicts.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did the United Nations (UN) fail to intervene to stop the Cambodian genocide?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The political interests of key Security Council members were better served by nonintervention.
The number of people killed did not meet the definition of genocide.
The United States was sympathetic to the goals of the Khmer Rouge regime.
No political group within Cambodia was willing to accept aid from the United Nations.

assessment_items.question_content:
The United Nations (UN) Charter establishes a responsibility for member nations to respond to acts of genocide. Despite this framework, the international community has repeatedly failed to intervene during mass killings.

**What reason is most often cited to justify this inaction?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Leaders cite national sovereignty, arguing that intervention violates a state’s autonomy.
Religious differences between conflicting groups are cited as a reason for inaction.
Economic concerns about the cost of intervention are cited as the primary reason for inaction.
A lack of credible evidence of mass violence is cited as the reason for inaction.

assessment_items.question_content:
>So let us challenge ourselves to build a world in which no branch of humanity, because of national, racial, ethnic, or religious origin, is again threatened with destruction because of those characteristics, of which people should rightly be proud. Let us work together as a community of civilized nations to strengthen our ability to prevent and, if necessary, to stop genocide.

>—U.S. President William Clinton, remarks following the Rwandan genocide

**Which 20th-century event and international response most closely reflects the sentiment expressed by President Clinton?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The international community’s promise of “never again” after failing to intervene during the Holocaust.
U.S. President George W. Bush’s “mission accomplished” announcement after the U.S.-led invasion of Iraq.
The collapse of the Berlin Wall marking the end of the Cold War.
The U.S. declaration of war after Japan’s attack on Pearl Harbor.

assessment_items.question_content:
Osama bin Laden’s attacks against the United States, including the World Trade Centers, were part of a larger strategy to weaken U.S. influence around the world. 

**Which of the following best explains that strategy?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He aimed to provoke a major U.S. military response that would drain its resources and damage its global standing.
He believed provoking the United States would lead to its withdrawal from global affairs.
His goal was to force the United States into an immediate surrender.
By provoking an overreaction, he hoped to disrupt the global economy and gain influence over international trade.

assessment_items.question_content:
>States like these, and their terrorist allies, constitute an axis of evil, arming to threaten the peace of the world. By seeking weapons of mass destruction, these regimes pose a grave and growing danger… In any of these cases, the price of indifference would be catastrophic.

>—U.S. President George W. Bush, “Axis of Evil” (2002)

**Which of the following identifies a significant global impact of this speech?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The U.S.-led 2003 invasion of Iraq.
Diplomatic breakthroughs with Iran and North Korea.
A declaration ending the War on Terror.
Stronger cooperation between the United States and the UN.

assessment_items.question_content:
On May 1, 2003, President George W. Bush stood aboard the USS Abraham Lincoln, in front of a banner reading “Mission Accomplished,” and declared the end of major combat operations in Iraq. Critics argue that this announcement was premature and misleading.

**Which of the following would best support the critics’ claim?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Iraq War officially ended in December 2011.
Saddam Hussein’s government had collapsed by 2003.
Iran agreed to halt its nuclear program after the invasion.
U.S. troops were withdrawn immediately following the announcement.

assessment_items.question_content:
Following the September 11 attacks in 2001, anti-Muslim hate crimes in the United States increased by 1,600%.

**Which of the following best explains what caused this spike?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Fear and stereotyping led many Americans to wrongly associate all Muslims with terrorism.
American Muslim groups attempted to incite conflict within the United States.
Large public protests by pro-Muslim groups encouraged hostility from the public.
Most Americans recognized that extremist attackers represented only a tiny fraction of Muslims.
